title: Chevron Houston Marathon 2025: A PR on the Bayou
content_md:
## A perfect day for a marathon

I woke up to 42°F and dead-calm air, which is about as good as it gets in Houston in January. By the time I hopped on the light rail to the George R. Brown Convention Center, the sky was already turning that pale, hopeful shade of blue you only see right before a long race.

### The start

The corrals at Chevron are *huge* — this is one of the largest marathons in the country, and you feel it. I lined up in Corral B and tried not to get swept up in the adrenaline. My A-goal was 3:25, my B-goal was anything starting with a 3.

The first miles unfold through downtown and Midtown, and the crowds are already three deep. I clicked off 7:45s and tried to keep my heart rate in check.

### Through the neighborhoods

The stretch through the Heights and Memorial is the soul of this race. Front yards become aid stations. There's a guy on Heights Boulevard who hands out tequila shots every year — I waved him off this time, but I noticed.

By mile 18 I was hurting and the wheels were threatening to come off. A pack of Houston Striders pulled me along through Memorial Park and saved my race.

### The finish

I rolled into the convention center at **3:23:48**, a 4-minute PR. The post-race food hall is a Houston tradition — warm broth, grilled cheese, and a plastic medal so big it makes the TSA nervous.

If you've never run Chevron, put it on your list. The course is fast, the city shows up, and the weather (usually) cooperates.

title: 50K Through the Alligators: Brazos Bend Trail Race
content_md:
## Why a 50K at a state park named after a river bend?

Brazos Bend State Park is forty-five minutes southwest of Houston and feels like a different planet. Spanish moss, hardwood swamps, slow-moving water, and yes — alligators sunning themselves on the levees like they pay rent.

The Brazos Bend 50 (which also offers a 50-mile and a 25K) loops through this terrain on packed dirt and crushed limestone. It's one of the flattest trail races in Texas, which sounds easy until you remember it's still 50 kilometers.

### Pre-dawn

We started at 7:00 AM in headlamp light, breath fogging in the 38°F cold. I'd seeded a drop bag at the lap aid station with a dry shirt, gels, and a small bag of sour gummy worms that became spiritually important around mile 22.

### The middle miles

The trail rolls past Elm Lake and 40-Acre Lake. On the second loop I counted six gators within twenty feet of the trail. None of them moved. I'm told they almost never do, but I still ran a little faster.

The miles after the marathon mark are where this race gets you. There's no climbing to break up your stride, no technical sections to demand your attention — just metronomic, identical footstrikes for hours. My quads started locking up around 28.

### The finish

I walked the last half-mile and stumbled across in **5:47**, well off my goal but happy. The post-race tacos at the finish line tent were maybe the best food I've ever eaten.

**Tips if you're thinking about it:**

- Train for flat. Sounds strange, but flat trail running uses your legs differently than rolling terrain. Practice it.
- Pack mosquito spray for the staging area.
- Don't underestimate how much the late-race monotony will mess with your head.

title: Spring Speed at Bayou City Classic
content_md:
## A first 10K worth waking up for

This was my first race longer than a 5K, and I picked Bayou City Classic because three different people at the BON Running Club told me it was the right one to start with. They were right.

### The course

The race starts and finishes at Sam Houston Park, just west of downtown, and runs out and back along Allen Parkway and the Buffalo Bayou greenway. It's almost completely flat — you barely climb 50 feet over the whole 10K — and the views of the skyline coming back into the finish are unreal.

### How it went

I'd been training for eight weeks and aiming for 55 minutes. The first mile felt embarrassingly easy at 8:30, so I tried to settle in. Around mile 3 I realized I was *way* ahead of my pacing plan and could either ride it or blow up.

I rode it. Crossed the finish line in **52:14** and immediately texted my mom.

### What I learned

- Don't go out too fast in your first 10K. (I got away with it. You probably won't.)
- The aid stations were well-spaced — one around mile 2, one around mile 4 — and the volunteers were genuinely cheerful at 7:30 AM, which I respect.
- Stay for the post-race kolaches. This is non-negotiable.

I signed up for a half marathon in November. The Bayou City Classic was the moment I realized I might actually be a runner now.
